SECTION L – INSTRUCTIONS TO OFFERORS

L.1 GENERAL INFORMATION

Solicitation Number: 693JJ3-24-R-000417
Issuing Agency: Federal Highway Administration (FHWA), Office of Acquisition and Grants Management
NAICS Code: 541330 – Engineering Services
Small Business Size Standard: $25.5M
Product Service Code: R425 – Support: Professional – Engineering/Technical

This procurement is a 100% HUBZone Set-Aside in accordance with FAR 19.1305. Only offerors that are certified by the Small Business Administration (SBA) as HUBZone small business concerns are eligible to submit proposals. Offerors must be a certified HUBZone small business concern at the time of proposal submission and must maintain HUBZone certification through contract award. The contracting officer will verify HUBZone status through the SBA's Dynamic Small Business Search (DSBS) prior to award.

The clause at FAR 52.219-13, Notice of Set-Aside of Orders (Nov 2020), is incorporated by reference. Additionally, the HUBZone price evaluation preference per FAR 19.1307 applies to this solicitation.

L.2 EVALUATION METHODOLOGY

The Government will evaluate proposals using a Best Value Tradeoff methodology. Proposals will be evaluated against the following factors: (1) Technical Capability, (2) Past Performance, and (3) Price. Technical Capability and Past Performance, when combined, are significantly more important than Price.

L.3 PROPOSAL SUBMISSION

Offerors shall submit proposals electronically via email to fhwa-acquisitions@dot.gov no later than 2:00 PM Eastern Time on April 18, 2025. Late submissions will be handled in accordance with FAR 15.208. All proposals shall consist of a Technical Volume (not to exceed 30 pages), a Past Performance Volume, and a Price Volume submitted as separate files.

SECTION L – INSTRUCTIONS, CONDITIONS, AND NOTICES TO OFFERORS

General Services Administration (GSA)
Public Buildings Service – Region 4
Solicitation Number: 47PA0425R0019

Project: Interior Renovation and Space Reconfiguration – Sam Nunn Atlanta Federal Center, Atlanta, GA

L.1 GENERAL INSTRUCTIONS

This solicitation is issued as a competitive set-aside for Women-Owned Small Business (WOSB) concerns in accordance with FAR Subpart 19.15, the Women-Owned Small Business Federal Contract Program. The applicable NAICS code for this procurement is 236220 – Commercial and Institutional Building Construction, with a size standard of $45.0 million.

All offerors must be SBA-certified Women-Owned Small Business concerns at the time of initial offer and must maintain that status through contract award. Offerors shall include as part of Volume I (Administrative) the following documentation:

a. A copy of the offeror's current WOSB certification from the SBA;
b. A representation that the offeror meets the definition of a WOSB concern per FAR 2.101;
c. A completed copy of FAR 52.219-1, Small Business Program Representations;
d. Business designation and status as reflected in the offeror's SAM.gov registration, including verification that the entity profile reflects Women-Owned Small Business (WOSB) status under the applicable NAICS code.

The solicitation incorporates the following set-aside-related clauses:
52.219-29 Notice of Set-Aside for, or Sole-Source Award to, Women-Owned Small Business Concerns Eligible Under the Women-Owned Small Business Program (Mar 2023)
52.219-14 Limitations on Subcontracting (Oct 2022)
52.219-8 Utilization of Small Business Concerns (Oct 2022)

Offers received from firms that are not verified as certified WOSB concerns will be rejected as nonresponsive. The Contracting Officer will verify WOSB status using SAM.gov and the SBA's certification database.

100% SDVOSB SET ASIDE

This contract action is subject to the Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) 6.302-5 Authorized or required by statute, Sole source awards under the Veterans Benefits Act of 2003 (15 U.S.C. 657f). AMENDMENT 0001 TO SOLICITATION NO. EPA-R8-2026-RFP-0087

Issuing Office: U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, Region 8, Denver, Colorado
Date of Amendment: April 14, 2026

The purpose of this amendment is to (1) respond to vendor questions received during the inquiry period, and (2) extend the proposal due date to May 2, 2026, at 5:00 PM Mountain Time.

All other terms and conditions of the original solicitation remain unchanged.

For reference, the following summary is restated from the original solicitation:

PROJECT SUMMARY

EPA Region 8 requires contractor support for the collection, digitization, and quality assurance of historical paper-based groundwater monitoring records associated with the Libby Asbestos Superfund Site in Lincoln County, Montana. Approximately 4,500 individual monitoring well data sheets, chain-of-custody forms, and field sampling logs dating from 1999 through 2014 must be scanned, indexed, and entered into EPA's SCRIBE environmental data management system.

The total contract value for this effort shall not exceed $85,000.00. The contract will be awarded as a time-and-materials type contract pursuant to FAR 16.601, with a ceiling price established at award. The contractor shall not exceed the ceiling price without prior written authorization from the Contracting Officer.

QUESTION AND ANSWER SUMMARY

Q1: Are the paper records currently organized chronologically or by monitoring well ID?
A1: Records are partially organized by monitoring well identification number but are not uniformly sorted. The contractor should anticipate initial sorting effort as part of the digitization task.

Q2: Will Government-furnished equipment (scanners, workstations) be available?
A2: No. The contractor shall provide all scanning equipment, software, and consumables. Workspace will be provided at the EPA Region 8 field office in Libby, Montana.

Challenge-Based Acquisition
ClaimsCore will be procured through a multi-phase challenge process. The objective is to create an environment where acquisition decisions are based on experimentation and demonstration of existing or emerging technology through a rapid and impactful assessment of commercial solutions and competing "prototypes" (i.e., limited implementations).

CMS contemplates multiple awards including a few contracts – perhaps as many as four or perhaps only one. Due to the operational cost and burden of a PoC, fewer awards are preferred. Accordingly, CMS has established an aggressive base-period (POC Phase) affordability objective. CMS encourages bidders to propose the best Proof of Concept solution possible at the lowest possible cost. Those vendors will begin work under a proof-of-concept (POC) phase where their solutions are tested against real CMS data and measured against CMS-established success criteria and metrics.

The challenge cycles in Phase 2 Option will provide quantitative evidence of the Proposers' abilities to develop a real-time, knowledge and safety maximizing AI Test Agents and will serve as the primary readiness criteria for flight tests. Phase 2 Option will culminate in a full-scale demonstration wherein the integrated CyPhERs will autonomously execute a test plan on an experimental aircraft. Direct comparisons will be drawn between the efficiency of the CyPhER-driven test plan and the conventional test plan previously executed on that aircraft.

The CyPhER Forge program metrics will use HFMs and flight test data from an experimental aircraft as benchmarks. Each challenge cycle shown in the program schedule will evaluate the metrics identified in Table 4, with progressive increases in scenario complexity, model fidelity and model speed requirements. To meet the goals of the program, Proposers are required to achieve the metrics identified in Table 4 and Table 5. The primary metric for program success is achieving a decrease by ten times (10X reduction) in the number of test points required to characterize key multi-physics quantities of interest to a specified global confidence interval, compared to the conventional test plan of the experimental aircraft.

Proposers successfully achieving objective targets for AI Test Agents in Phase 2 Option, will introduce adaptive test planning based on rigorous measures of knowledge gain subject to user constraints, and provide statistical safety guarantees including low false alarm rate per maneuver, low missed alarm rate per maneuver, and highly accurate safety violation probability characterization. This will far surpass the state-of-the-art in test safety and planning, which is driven by historical heuristics and fixed intervals.

The program will decompose requirements into epics, capabilities, and user stories, ensuring that development teams can deliver working software at a rapid cadence aligning with SAFe Agile methodology. • Assistance in the prioritization and planning of software work items within DSCA Product Service Delivery Framework, as part of managing one or more software backlogs.
• Development of Solutions Architectures which utilize emerging and/or disruptive technologies (e.g. Artificial Intelligence (AI))
• Developing web-applications, APIs, and other software consistent with concurrently developed emergent architectures.
• Developing software documentation
• Developing and implementing an improved, modern data architecture while maintaining interim compatibility with a to-be-retired database

7.1.3 Requirements Analysis/Requirements Management Support
The Contractor shall provide support for capturing, analyzing, and managing LA workstreams/projects requirements. The level of support required is estimated to be one (1) Requirements Analyst/Requirements Manager (RA/RM) FTE. The Contractor shall support LA workstream/projects in capturing and managing requirements from identification to implementation and validation in accordance with IRS Requirements Engineering Program Office (REPO) requirements management methodology and standards. The Contractor shall:
• Plan, coordinate, and lead requirements capture workshops, focus groups, surveys, and other requirements elicitation activities.
• Define approach/methodology for gathering and capturing business and system solution requirements.
• Analyze, decompose, and document business requirements.
• For Agile projects, document requirements in the Agile Epics/Stories structure (who, what, why).
• Analyzing enterprise level financial, data, operational, infrastructure, security, IPV6, and Section 508 requirements and determine applicability to LA workstream/project.
• Manage requirements throughout the project life cycle using IRS Enterprise Life-cycle Management (ELM) tool.
• Trace requirements throughout the various phases (e.g., design, implementation, testing) of the project.
• Document use cases and data flows.
• Develop and document requirements implementation acceptance criteria.
• Support development and review of test cases and ensure that test cases are adequate constructed to validate that requirements are met.
• Perform other requirements management activities as may be necessary to meet workstream/project needs.

A 'brand name or equal' purchase description cannot be used because no other commercially available product offers an equivalent, fully integrated solution that meets all required technical and operational criteria. Attempting to define 'equal' characteristics would either be overly restrictive—effectively describing only Security Onion—or too broad, resulting in solutions that lack critical capabilities or require extensive modification. In either case, the Government would incur increased costs, schedule delays, and technical risk associated with integrating multiple disparate tools to approximate the required functionality. Market research conducted across Department of War Enterprise Software Initiative (DoW ESI), National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) Solutions for Enterprise-Wide Procurement (SEWP), and SAM.gov confirmed that while other cybersecurity and analytics platforms exist, none provide the same combination of capabilities in a unified, mission-aligned solution. No authorized reseller was identified that could deliver the complete requirement, including software, licensing, and certified training. Furthermore, similar products either lack full packet capture integration, require significant customization to achieve comparable functionality, or depend on additional third-party components and licensing structures. These limitations prevent such products from meeting the Government's needs without substantial modification, which would introduce unacceptable cost, schedule, and performance risks. Accordingly, the specific brand name Security Onion Solutions, LLC, is essential to meet the Government's minimum requirements. The use of a brand name specification is therefore justified, as no other product can meet the requirement without compromising mission effectiveness, increasing risk, or requiring fundamental changes to the Government's operational objectives.

Title: Digital Content Evaluation and Analysis Platform License Subscription for CDC Office of Communication

SECTION 1 – BACKGROUND

The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) Office of Communication (OC) routinely evaluates digital content and design to present timely, relevant and actionable health information to the general public, public health and healthcare professionals. This type of evaluation includes user testing which is the testing of digital content with actual end users, using their feedback to make improvements. This is conducted by showing users digital content or prototypes and taking notes as they conduct a range of task-based scenarios.

This type of evaluation has been historically difficult to accomplish as the OC UX staff needs to be able to watch users' desktop or mobile screens in order to document and determine their success in completing user tasks. While software exists to share desktop screens, we need a platform that can easily share mobile screens. This poses a problem as CDC has a very large mobile audience. In addition, OC UX staff has had to spend a tremendous amount of time recruiting users to participate in user testing activities, as well as conducting each user testing session and taking detailed notes to track every user action.

Two existing and well used models were identified when developing the GOLD Standard. They are:
• Expected Progeny Differentials (EPDs): Used for decades in the hog and cattle industries to evaluate individual breeding stocks, relative to their peers within their breed, instead of individual analyses
• Corn Suitability Rating (CSR): A singular composite rating and scoring system used to evaluate different tracts of land for sales to better equip buyers with relevant information on future purchases

KEY FEATURES AND DESIGN PRINCIPALS:
• Simplicity and Transparency: Avoids overwhelming users with excessive metrics; delivers one interpretable score with clear explanations that matter to CUSTOMERS/USERS and fosters trust by enabling quick decisions.
• Integration: Complements existing workflows, using lab/NIR data inputs and supporting—not replacing—crude protein, as the transitional benchmark.
• Adoption-Focused: Phased validation, governance and education via pilots, workshops and an adoption playbook. Data remains customer-owned, with secure handling
• Future-Proofing: Modular for expansion and modification like outcome validation and automated data ingestion, without complicating the core experience.

Supply chain risks range from natural threats to counterfeit products and apply to quality, security, resiliency, and product integrity. Mitigation plans to manage risks may involve logistics, cybersecurity, and financial management. The goal is to preclude undue risk to the Government and ensure supply chain continuity in the event normal business operations are interrupted. The offeror's SCRM plan must include countermeasures which mitigate threats and system vulnerabilities, including:

Funding has not yet been encumbered for this initiative. MAC Onboarding
CMS intends to onboard MAC jurisdictions incrementally in production using a run-out strategy. For each MAC (or MAC region), CMS will designate a cutover Date of Service (DOS), and inbound claims routing will be based on DOS (not receipt date). Claims with a DOS prior to the cutover DOS will continue to be routed to, and processed by, the legacy environment with the legacy environment remaining the system of record for those pre-cutover services. Claims with a DOS on or after the cutover DOS will be routed to and adjudicated by ClaimsCore, with ClaimsCore serving as the system of record for those post-cutover services.

Incremental MAC onboarding will create a temporary "split-history" operating state in which a beneficiary's claims history and benefit accumulators are distributed across ClaimsCore and the Legacy Systems. During this hybrid period, beneficiaries may receive care in jurisdictions that have not yet cut over, jurisdictions processing on ClaimsCore, or both. Adjudication in either environment may require timely access to claim outcomes and beneficiary liability information that resides in the other environment (e.g., prior payments and denials, duplicate or overlapping service checks, utilization and frequency limits, deductible and coinsurance accumulation).

Specific places of performance will be identified in individual Task Orders issued under the contract. The contractor shall comply with host nation agreements for performance of any work in OCONUS locations and as specified in individual Task Orders.

7. Hours of Operation
The contractor must maintain an adequate workforce for the uninterrupted performance of all tasks to be defined within each task order.

11. Status of Forces Agreements (SOFA)
It is possible for some contract activity to occur at OCONUS locations. In the event such support is required, if the country in which contractor support will occur has a SOFA with the United States, it is critical for those designated for travel to receive authority to operate as a contractor for the U.S. Military in those countries. The Government will identify SOFA points of contact (POCs) for any OCONUS location where the contractor will be required to travel. The contractor shall coordinate to understand fully and to complete and submit necessary documents to the SOFA authority for each country where such support is to be scheduled. The contractor shall perform this task not later than 90 calendar days prior to any scheduled travel.
